behaviors.description: Migrations are applied in ascending numeric order determined by the filename prefix.
behaviors.justification: Failure is at module handoff (file discovery -> numeric sort -> execution order), not a single pure function. A unit test of the sorter alone can miss wiring bugs (e.g., unsorted iteration passed to executor). No real OS/network/subprocess behavior is required beyond local filesystem/DB wiring, and no AI/ML dependency exists.
behaviors.test_description: Run runner with migration files like `1_init.sql`, `2_add.sql`, `10_late.sql` and assert executed order is 1,2,10 via recorded execution log or DB side effects proving sequence.

behaviors.description: A migration that has already been applied is not re-executed on a subsequent run.
behaviors.justification: This is idempotency across runs via `_migrations` state handoff (runner <-> metadata table), so higher than Unit. It does not require real OS/network/subprocess-specific behavior; in-process module wiring with local DB/test DB is sufficient. No LLM dependency.
behaviors.test_description: Execute runner twice on same schema; verify second run performs no migration SQL for already-recorded versions and leaves data/schema unchanged except expected metadata.

behaviors.description: When a migration statement fails, its changes are rolled back automatically and no further migrations in the same run are attempted.
behaviors.justification: Behavior text requires real transactional behavior: "rolled back automatically". Mocked transaction objects can falsely pass while real Postgres transaction/statement semantics fail. This is REAL-INFRA (real DB engine/process behavior), so Integration is too low. No AI/ML dependency.
behaviors.test_description: Against real Postgres, run migrations where one file partially mutates state then triggers SQL error; assert prior statements in that migration are absent after run (rollback) and later migration files were not attempted.

behaviors.description: When two instances of the runner start at the same time against the same database, each migration is applied exactly once across both instances.
behaviors.justification: Behavior explicitly requires concurrent execution from "two instances" and correctness under cross-process race conditions. That failure boundary is subprocess scheduling + real DB locking/constraints; mocks often hide races. No LLM dependency.
behaviors.test_description: Launch two real runner processes concurrently against same Postgres and shared migrations; assert each version appears once in `_migrations` and resulting schema/data reflects single application per migration.

behaviors.description: On the very first run against a fresh database, the `_migrations` table is created before any migration file is applied.
behaviors.justification: This validates initialization ordering across components (bootstrap metadata table -> apply files). It is not purely local logic, so above Unit. Real subprocess/OS behavior is not required for the core failure; local DB wiring is sufficient. No AI/ML dependency.
behaviors.test_description: Start with fresh DB lacking `_migrations`; run runner and assert `_migrations` is created before first migration execution (e.g., via query hooks/log timestamps or intentional first migration that depends on metadata presence).
